The upside from today’s price is barely 40 cents, but we must remember that there are multiple tailwinds that are pushing the business’s growth. I believe that if the business performs even remotely close to those topline expectations, it is very likely that the market will quickly re-rate its sales multiple to the 3.5 or 4x range, and that represents nearly 100% upside from the $5.8/share it is trading at now.

Also, the company isn’t too far off breakeven and eventual profitability. Given what the last few quarters have been like, I’ll stick my neck out and say that it can be profitable as soon as the middle of FY27. It could be sooner, but I wouldn’t be so sure. If that happens, I’m very confident that the market will start assigning a premium to the stock’s price.

The Risks To My Position

I think the most obvious risk to any bullishness around Telos is its customer concentration. I touched on this before, but I will reiterate it again. The company’s entire business is basically built around federal government contracts and awards, for the most part. That is a moat, yes, but it is also risky, as the government shutdown and policies like tariffs have shown us. That volatility is a big part of why the stock has traded so cheaply in recent years, and the higher the value, the more important it is that it has some level of earnings stability. That should come with the ID side of its business, so I’m not too bothered at this time.

Revenue lumpiness is next, and it ties into my previous point. We saw the ramp in revenue when Security Solutions started picking up, especially in Q3, but the dynamic cuts both ways. One major program going offline will dent the company’s revenue outlook, and it needs to keep working to add more customers from the billion-dollar pipeline that management is so bullish on.

The segments also need to balance out. Right now, the revenue mix between Security Solutions and Security Networks is 90:10, and that is working out. But it also means that Telos is relying on a fairly narrow set of growth drivers, so any dip in the former’s growth trajectory will be particularly impactful if Security Networks doesn’t start picking up some slack.

I mentioned that Q3 FY25 was the company’s best quarter in a long time in terms of operating margins, but some of that improvement came from easier YoY comparisons. The real test now is whether those margins will hold up if the volumes fluctuate and new work comes in under different contract structures. It’s not a risk I can discount because it plays a big role in how we price the stock moving forward.

The last risk I must highlight is the valuation itself. The stock has already rallied very nicely, and the market expects that the company will continue performing at the current levels. So, Telos doesn’t need to miss badly for us to see the downside. One or two “okay” quarters might just do it.

Final Thoughts

Telos has done the hard part, and it is recovering nicely in terms of its financials. I also like its strategy and the tailwinds that will power it, so the question is whether it can grow into the valuation the market seems to be expecting. I believe it will, especially if it just gives us a few more quarters of consistent execution, so I’m willing to have some faith in the upside and rate the stock a Buy.

data.title: RIV: Fund Of Funds, 13% Yield, Distribution Hike
data.description:
We previously covered RiverNorth Opportunities Fund (RIV), a closed-end fund, in an article in early September 2025. At that time, we rated RIV a Hold due to a history of distribution declines and lagging performance.

However, on 1/2/26, RIV raised its monthly distribution by 2.3%, from $.1277 to $.1306, its first distribution hike since January 2024. At its 1/22/26 closing price of $11.89, RIV yields a hefty 13.18%. Its next two monthly ex-dividend dates are 2/13/26 and 3/16/26.

Fund Profile

"The Fund employs a tactical asset allocation strategy primarily comprised of closed-end funds, exchange-traded funds, special purpose acquisition companies (SPACs), and business development companies (BDCs). RiverNorth implements an opportunistic investment strategy designed to capitalize on the inefficiencies in the closed-end fund space while simultaneously providing diversified exposure to several asset classes." (RIV site)

RIV's net base increased from $275M at 6/30/25, to $343M as of 1/21/26, due to a rights offering, which sold 5,899,015 new shares of common stock at a subscription price of $11.95. Its expense ratio was steady, while leverage decreased from 32% to 24.42% as of 1/21/26. Quantity of holdings was up slightly, from 339 to 341, and average daily volume was 94K. RIV incepted in December 2015:

Holdings

As of 12/31/25, special purpose acquisition companies, "SPACs", remained the top exposure, rising from 26% to 28%, with fixed income declining from 19% to 16%, and US equities steady, at 20%. Investment company bonds dropped from 13% to 12%, while international equities rose from 6% to 7%.

The biggest shift in assets came in BDCs, which rose from 2% to 6%, as of 12/31/25 - a contrarian move, with rates generally forecast to decline.

CEFs dropped from 45% at 6/30/25 to 43% as of 12/31/25, with investment company debt up from 13% to 14%, while other categories remained steady.

As of 9/30/25, the US was by far RIV's main regional exposure, at ~63% in equity and 28% in fixed income. It held minor positions in several other nations, running from 0.32% for Germany up to 1.32% for Hong Kong:

RIV's portfolio remained focused upon investment grade securities, but at a lower rate, as AAA - BBB-rated holdings comprised ~58% of the portfolio, vs. 63% as of 3/31/25. RIV also held ~23% in unrated securities, plus ~15.8% in non-investment grade securities:

And 3-5 year maturities still had the highest weighting, at 11.87%, vs. 12.36% at 3/31/25, while 10-20 year maturities declined from 10.24% to 5.37% as of 9/30/25. 1-3 year maturities fell from 5.25% to 3.5%.

As of 9/30/25, RIV's top 10 holdings were still headed by Bill Ackman's Pershing Square Holdings, at 12.66%. It's "structured as a closed-ended fund that principally holds concentrated positions in large capitalization companies." (Pershing site)

The iShares Flexible Income & Opportunities ETF, which mainly invests in debt and income-producing securities, rose from 5% on 6/30/25 to 5.8% as of 9/30/25.

Saba Capital Income and Opportunities Fund, at 3.83% as of 6/30/25, was replaced in the Top 10 by Saba Capital Income and Opportunities Fund II, at 2.8%. Also gone from the top 10 were Nuveen Municipal Value Fund, Clough Global Opportunities Fund, and Calamos Long/Short Equity & Dynamic Income Trust.

New additions to the top 10 were Howard Hughes Holdings, Royce Value Trust, and Royce Micro Cap Trust, in a notable move toward smaller cap equities.

Earnings

RIV's fiscal year ends on 6/30. NII rose by ~3% in fiscal year ended 6/30/25. Net realized gains swung from a $6M loss to a $24.94M gain, while unrealized gains declined from a $38.28M gain to a $1.82M loss. NII, realized gains, and long-term gains covered 95% of common distributions in fiscal year ended 6/30/25, a marked improvement over the period ended 6/30/24, when coverage was nil, after accounting for preferred distributions.

Taxes

Management estimated that 45% of 2024 distributions were non-qualified, and 49% were non-dividend distributions, i.e., a return of capital. ROC offers a tax deferral, but it reduces your tax basis, which will impact your tax liability if you sell your shares.

Performance

As of 12/31/25, RIV had outperformed the Morningstar US CEF category over the past year. It lagged in price over the past three-, five- and 10-year periods but outperformed the category on an NAV basis:

RIV outperformed the category on an NAV basis in 2025, rising 18.18%, and also did so in 2023 and 2021. It also held up better in 2022 on both a price and NAV basis.

Risks

Interest rate risk: As seen above, rising interest rates pose a risk to RIV due to its exposure to BDCs - it fell 11% in price and NAV in 2022. Its fixed income exposure also has risks associated with rising rates.

SPAC risk: SPACs pose significant risks for investors, in that investors don't know which companies these entities may acquire. There can be a rapid timeline for mergers, which may lead to bad deals. In addition, there's less regulatory control over SPACs. Overall, SPACs have had a mixed record.

Valuations

Buying CEFs at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy due to mean reversion. CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day. As of the 1/21/26 close, RIV was trading at a 5.58% discount to its daily NAV/share of $12.55,which was a much deeper discount than its five-year average 1.94% discount. It was a similar discount to its three-year average of 5.53%, and a bit deeper than its one-year average of 4.26%:

Parting Thoughts

While RIV can offer you diversification via its holdings in many disparate assets, we advise waiting for a deeper discount to NAV. Its 52-week discount to NAV ranged as deep as 9.92%.

All tables are furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: Xiaomi Suffers EV/Smartphone Headwinds - Oversold Indicators Imply Trading Floor
data.description:
I previously covered Xiaomi Corporation (XIACY) (XIACF) in October 2025, discussing its robust growth prospects from the diversified ecosystem across the smart devices/EVs and the upcoming EV profitability.

Combined with the attractive valuations despite the triple-digit rally in 2024, the growing backlog/expanding production capacity, and the healthy balance sheet, I had initiated a Buy rating upon a moderate retracement then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the XIACY stock here, thanks to the improved margin of safety from the recent meltdown and the notably cheaper valuations compared to historical trends and its automaker/smartphone peers.

This is aided by their ability to weather the potential EV/smartphone downturn in 2026, attributed to their growing Free Cash Flow generations and the richer balance sheet, despite the risks from the lowered domestic EV tax exemption/trade-in subsidies in 2026, the intensifying domestic/international EV competition, and the impact of the memory chip supply crunch on its smartphone prospects.

XIACY Faces Near-Term EV Headwinds

For now, XIACY has unfortunately breached my prior Buy Zone at the intermediate support levels of $32s to retest the 2025 trading floor of $22s by the time of writing, with a similar pessimism also observed in its Chinese automaker peers in varying degrees.

The headwinds are partly attributed to:

  • the transition from full purchase tax exemption in 2025 to a 50% exemption from January 01, 2026 onwards - with "NEV buyers will pay 5% purchase tax starting this year,"
  • the further reduction to 25% exemption from January 01, 2027 onwards - 10% purchase tax, and
  • the transition from fixed-amount subsidies in 2025 to percentage-based subsidies relative to the vehicle price in 2026,

with it expected to trigger "a short-term sales surge as buyers rush to beat the deadline, followed by a potential slowdown in early 2026 once the reduced benefit takes effect."

The increase in China's EV retail sales has been observed at 1.337M units (+1.2% MoM/+2.6% YoY) in December 2025 along with 12.81M units in 2025 (+17.6% YoY), albeit with things notably cooling for the first three weeks of 2026 at -52% MoM/-16% YoY.

The same has been observed in XIACY's guidance, at which there may be a certain impact on its gross margins in FY2026, with interested investors well advised to monitor their future execution:

And regarding the vacancy tax, regarding next year's ASP and our gross margin, there will be certain impact. However, our current gross margin is at a healthy level. So we hope that while we can maintain or guarantee our delivery, we can still maintain a healthy gross margin.

(Xiaomi - FQ3'25 earnings call)

My caution is attributed to XIACY competitors' intensified discounting strategies, with it remaining to be seen if the former may join the potential price war if demand remains lackluster:

As deliveries failed to live up to expectations, all major players would have to slash prices of their vehicles to reduce their inventories at the beginning of 2026... Competition is getting fiercer this year because of weaker demand for new cars.

(South China Morning Post)

With XIACY set to report their FQ4'25 earnings call sometime in March 2026, I urge readers to closely monitor their upcoming performance and the management's forward commentary indeed.

This is because it is uncertain if the prior FQ3'25 Smart EV/AI segment profitability at gross margins of ~25.5% (+8.4 points YoY) and operating margins at approximately ~2.4% (+19.9 points YoY) may be replicated from FY2026 onwards.

This is especially since many of XIACY's competitors have highlighted their international EV growth ambitions to mitigate the maturing domestic market, with the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers expecting "vehicle exports are likely to rise 4.3% after a stronger-than-expected 21.1% growth in 2025."

Given XIACY's somewhat late global EV entrance from 2027 onwards and the potential domestic inventory glut at a time of mixed demand environment, I can understand why the management has offered a relatively conservative FY2026 EV delivery guidance of 550K units (+37.5% YoY).

2026 Brings Forth Smartphone Headwinds

Otherwise, readers must note that XIACY's main monetization avenue remains in the Smartphone segment at 40.6% of its overall revenues in FQ3'25 (-10.7 points YoY), with the ongoing data center memory supply crunch expected to trigger a downstream headwind in the supply and prices of smartphone memory chips.

For reference, International Data Corporation [IDC] previously reported that "for a mid-range device, memory can represent 15-20% of the total bill of materials (BOM), while for a high-end flagship device, it is around 10-15%," with these presenting headwinds to many smartphone OEMs, XIACY included.

My caution is attributed to the fact that XIACY is the third largest smartphone player globally with a market share of 13% (-1 point YoY) and the fourth largest smartphone player in China with a market share of 15.7% in 2025 (+0.7 points YoY).

While XIACY has been embarking on an intensified premiumization strategy in China, one that has been rather successful given that "our premium smartphone units sold accounted for 24.1% of our total smartphone units sold in the Chinese Mainland" (+4.1 points YoY), the same has not occurred overseas, as observed in the "decreased ASP in the overseas markets."

For now, XIACY already expects to mitigate some "cost pressure through product mix upgrades, ASP increases" while "digesting" some costs internally, with it likely to trigger near-term gross margin headwinds and, potentially, demand headwinds:

Regarding smartphone retail price, if it increases, then our judgment is that there will be a downturn in the overall smartphone market. That's my overall judgment. But how much? Well, of course, I can't really quantify in detail now. Now for smartphones, it is still a retention market for the time being.

(Xiaomi - FQ3'25 earnings call)

As a result of the potential top/bottom-line deterioration from the intensifying domestic EV competition, the delayed overseas EV launch, and the elevated memory prices impacting its smartphone prospects, it is unsurprising that XIACY has already lost -43.7% of its value since the 52-week highs.

XIACY Remains A Great Buy Here

For now, XIACY has already lost much of its 2025 stock price gains, with the stock currently retesting the support levels of $22s by the time of writing after running away from the 50/100/200 day moving averages.

It goes without saying that XIACY has already entered oversold territories by the time of writing, as observed in the trading volumes retesting prior 2021 lows.

The same has been observed in XIACY's discounted FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.56x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 19.08x, compared to the 1Y mean of 2.17x/28.90x and the sector median of 3.58x/25.28x, respectively.

When comparing XIACY's FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.56x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 19.08x to its smartphone/automaker peers, including:

  • Apple (AAPL) at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 8.01x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 30.09x,
  • Samsung (SSNLF) at 2.32x/25.35x,
  • Tesla (TSLA) at 15.50x/275.08x,
  • Li Auto (LI) at 0.34x/56.03x,
  • BYD Company Ltd. (BYDDY)(BYDDF) at 0.91x/21.77x,
  • XPeng (XPEV) at 1.46x/NA, and
  • NIO Inc. (NIO) at 1.10x/NA, respectively,

It is apparent that XIACY remains attractively valued at current levels, after the steep meltdown from recent 52 week highs, especially aided by the well-diversified Human x Car x Home smart ecosystem and the profitable growth trends.

These reasons may also be why the management has announced an outsized share repurchase program worth approximately ~$321M in early January 2026, as they acknowledge the cheap stock prices and deliver a robust shareholder return.

As a result of the potential bottoming at the $22s arising from the oversold technical indicators, the cheap valuations, and their ability to weather the near-term EV/smartphone headwinds thanks to the growing Free Cash Flow generation of 20.52B RMB on a YTD basis (+143.7% YoY) and the rich balance sheet with an estimated net cash position of 63.17B RMB (+22.3% YoY), I am reiterating my Buy rating for the XIACY stock here.

Much of the pessimism may already be baked in here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Forget 'Sell America': Davos 2026 Is About Geo-Capex Investing
data.description:
Investing After Davos 2026

This week, the World Economic Forum Annual Meeting (Davos) was at the center of the stage, and all eyes and ears were on President Trump's speech, waiting to understand what he would say about Greenland. We all know that the situation, from ice to a thaw when NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte announced that he and the U.S. President had reached a framework for a deal. There have been many comments on the subject,

Here, however, I would like to stick to what this year's Davos suggested to us as investors. After closely following the main events that were held in the highest town in Switzerland, I think a few facts should be clearly outlined, as I believe they will become part of our (economic) world.

Macroeconomic Snapshot

First of all, allow me to identify three things that simply looked strange as Davos started.

  1. Davos' title was "A Spirit of Dialogue," which immediately felt awkward, if not naive, as many Western political leaders talked about coordination, while preparing to face President Trump's willingness to control Greenland.
  2. European leaders seemed united (it usually isn't so) and even had to admit that Europe has to focus on its strategic autonomy, acknowledging that European countries will have to invest in it.
  3. Before Trump's speech, the market sold off, and a call to "Sell America" almost went viral through the news. Whatever we may think about the geopolitical tension, it simply feels off to sell America without any economically grounded reason.

Let me spend a few words on the "Sell America" trade. Indeed, we saw a quick rotation out of U.S. assets, with the Danish pension fund selling $100M worth of Treasuries. Now, even though this is a minuscule portion of the national debt, the political message was clear about a trust in the U.S. at decade lows among some of its Western allies. Gold, in the meantime, hit new ATHs. But these days, this is almost no news. We already saw together that indeed capital markets are repricing the risk associated with the U.S., which implies that the U.S. dollar and U.S. Treasuries are not considered risk-free assets anymore because of their bond with geopolitics. Gold, on the other hand, is politics-free. But the "Sell America" narrative didn't convince me at the start of the week. To say it bluntly, it has never convinced me, and I think it would take a big catastrophe for me to start believing it. I am not saying that everything is perfect in America.

But, as far as I see it, I see no better environment for businesses to thrive and for the economy to grow. Europe is surely an extremely interesting region, and I own top-notch European stocks, too (Ferrari and Prysmian are two of my holdings that many of my followers know well by now). I also believe that a trade war between the two areas of the West would be detrimental, but I think that flying to gold or shorting Treasuries are short-term strategies that don't allow investors to benefit from the huge strength of many American businesses that continue to thrive and compound at incredible growth rates. As soon as Trump said that he would not have invaded Greenland and that the deal framework was announced, we saw that the "Sell America" trade was just a positioning strategy and not that regime shift that many were describing on the news. Now, if it weren't for the people involved in the U.S.-Denmark-Greenland clash, I would have hoped the sell-off to last a bit longer to take advantage of this situation.

This leads me to a first consideration, which is a behavioral one. We can check how we behaved before Trump spoke. When we saw our assets go down, did we do anything? Did we sell? Did we hedge? If we did, I would hint that we might not have been truly comfortable with our holdings. I think the best test for our portfolio management skills is to see whether we are happy that prices are dropping. If we are happy to buy assets at a lower price, it means we are truly convinced of the value of our holdings.

Resulting Investing Themes

Many will say that it has never been clearer that defense stocks will do well. I think so, too. But this also seems a bit too simplistic. Besides, there are many people who don't want to hold defense stocks in their portfolios for several reasons, some of which are ethical.

However, I think that the real investing theme is not directly about war or defense. We have understood that what is going on with Greenland is not a joke. It is a matter of great importance because of its arctic routes, its key role in the Golden Dome project, its rare earths, and its strategic position for military logistics.

Before I move on to my key idea, allow me to talk a bit about Ukraine and Gaza. In Ukraine, in particular, I didn't hear any political leader say that the war there will be over soon. In addition, even if the war were to end, I always hear politicians talk about a multi-year reconstruction process. Ukraine, in other words, is a long-term balance sheet opportunity. Gaza, to some extent, anticipates what Ukraine could see. In Ukraine, for example, we are seeing that centralized infrastructure can be truly vulnerable because it offers the enemy a simple way to put pressure on a country. When Ukraine is rebuilt, new grid designs will be needed. This is what I would call "defensive-capex".

Some time ago, I wrote an article titled "Capex Nation: Investment Themes For A Decade". In an attempt to follow where money was flowing into, I built, so to speak, a geography of what was going on in the U.S. because of the manufacturing reshoring, the strategic needs to control chips, the investments in AI, and similar topics.

Now, after Davos, I believe that idea, which I have pursued and developed through my research through 2025, is even deeper. Capex is now linked not only to a domestic strategy. We have to view geography as capex.

Discussions over territories such as Greenland (or the Chagos Islands, to name another hot topic), Ukraine, Gaza, or Venezuela show us that geography is no longer a matter of maps and logistics. It is the newest and largest item in capex budgets. This is a major shift. After the fall of the Berlin Wall and the era of globalization, capex was not driven by geography, but by efficiency. When we heard about companies that were outsourcing and building factories in China, it was because they were looking for cheap labor. In 2026, capex is not driven by efficiency because it has become a matter of security. In other words, countries want to find the safest places to build their factories and to find power, while shortening as much as possible the supply chain they need. This is why we see something that was unthinkable just a few years ago: capex is now used to duplicate key assets because they have to be moved to safer places.

Geo-capex also implies that physical locations can become true bottlenecks that could attract many investors. I believe this is the reason why European leaders are accepting higher investments (=capex) due to security reasons. In particular, some geographies are almost forcing countries to spend. Greenland is the perfect example. Although it is a hostile region with few inhabitants, it is the most recent area that is attracting and generating massive investments both in defense systems and in infrastructure. Let's pause here for a moment. We know Greenland will play a key role in the Golden Dome project. But this doesn't only imply that some Patriots will be installed there. We will see new grids, new power generators, new engineering solutions, heavy industrial operations, and so on. This is the perfect environment for incumbent companies with the benefit of scale to thrive (I am thinking about Caterpillar, for example).

Secondly, geo-capex also means that borders will attract big investments. This is also a big shift. A few years ago, borders were fled because of their instability. Now, they are magnets for capex.

Third, Davos stressed what we have also been discussing for some time: energy security is a priority, and it is about capex, not about access to key commodities.

Geography as capex is therefore what explains why industrials, energy infrastructure, and utilities could be the winners and could deserve higher multiples compared to the ones that the previous narrative about their cyclicality implied.

A Few Names Worth Looking At

This article wants to be a broad coverage of an event that is making me better understand the market. As such, there is no place to cover in detail a single stock. However, after having explained what I better understood as Davos unfolded, I think that there are some interesting stocks that we should consider.

Surely, we could look at the supply chain that the Golden Dome requires. Here, some may think of companies such as Lockheed Martin (LMT) and RTX Corp (RTX). After all, the Pentagon recently announced a contract vehicle with a $151B total ceiling. I think the supply chain here has some hidden stocks, that are not yet fully discussed among investors. One is Kratos (KTOS), which builds aerial target drones. In other words, it produces the drones that are used by the military to replicate the behavior of hostile missiles so that they can train on how to shoot them down. The company recently announced that it will open a new 55,000 sqft manufacturing facility in Maryland to respond to the $1.4B contract that it has won. A second play could be Parsons Corporation (PSN), which already manages the infrastructure of the American base in Greenland. Parsons also recently won a contract with the Missile Defense Agency [MDA]. Finally, we could also try to take advantage of the Department of Defense's recent announcement that it will invest $1B in L3Harris' Missile Solutions business to help it spin off its rocket motor business.

There are also other ways to follow these trends. One is surely to invest in midstream operators (AMLP) and benefit from the increasing volumes of gas and oil that will be transported around the States toward the Gulf. But we could also look favorably at Canadian railroads, Canadian Pacific (CP) in particular, as it connects the whole continent and could benefit from the nearshoring trend. A grid builder, such as Quanta Services (PWR) or a strategic company in logistics, such as Prologis (PLD), could be two interesting stocks to have on our watchlist. Finally, Vertiv (VRT) has to be included, as liquid cooling and power management are two key aspects that enable the new data centers to operate.

Among European stocks, I would surely keep considering ASML, but I would also consider stocks such as Prysmian (PRYMY; PRYMF) or NKT (NRKBY; NRKBY).

Conclusion

I like soft water and SaaS companies. But this doesn't mean I am all in on them. I have always thought that hard asset ownership, although sometimes costly, keeps being interesting. In a world that is more and more digital, physical assets are actually scarcer and scarcer, thus increasing their value. Davos 2026 won't be remembered for its "Sell America" trade, but as a key moment where it became clear that geography is the new capex as the era of globalization without frictions belongs to the past. The stock ideas I mentioned were not explicitly discussed at Davos 2026. However, from Davos I was helped in shaping the mindset that allowed me to come up with these names. Capex is not following security needs, but rather for efficiency reasons. It is geography that now decides how expensive the bill will be.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The AI Winner Nobody Is Talking About: Self-Storage REITs
data.description:
I think that one of the most overlooked beneficiaries of the AI revolution is self-storage REITs, and there are three reasons why.

First, it is expected to lead to major job market disruption over the coming years, and this has historically been one of the strongest demand drivers for self-storage space.

As people lose their jobs, they often need to move elsewhere to find a new one, leading to demand for storage space. At the same time, some of these people may decide to downsize their residence to save money, creating additional need for storage space, and some of these businesses may need to store the extra stuff that's no longer needed, at least temporarily.

And this is just now starting to happen on a large scale. Just consider the magnitude of these recent layoff announcements:

  1. UPS: 48,000 employees

  2. Amazon: Up to 30,000 employees

  3. Intel: 24,000 employees

  4. Nestle: 16,000 employees

  5. Accenture: 11,000 employees

  6. Ford: 11,000 employees

  7. Novo Nordisk: 9,000 employees

  8. Microsoft: 7,000 employees

  9. PwC: 5,600 employees

  10. Salesforce: 4,000 employees

  11. Paramount: 2,000 employees

  12. Target: 1,800 employees

  13. Kroger: 1,000 employees

  14. Applied Materials: 1,444 employees

  15. Meta: 600 employees

(Source: The Kobeissi Letter)

The 2025 announced layoffs are nearly on par with the levels experienced during the Great Financial Crisis:

I think that this is a huge potential catalyst for self-storage REITs.

Every time someone moves, demand for self-storage spikes. Therefore, if AI leads to job disruption, I would expect more demand for self-storage.

Second, AI is also making it easier than ever before for anyone to start a new small business. It is breaking barriers to entry in most industries, allowing even solo entrepreneurs to now compete with larger companies.

Just think of an e-commerce business, for example. Before AI, you had to invest considerable amounts of time, money, and effort just to get your website and ads ready. Now, you can have all of it ready in a few hours at barely any cost. The same applies to customer service, logistics, legal, accounting, etc. Everything is becoming a lot easier, cheaper, and faster thanks to AI.

This is happening right as a lot of bright and ambitious people will lose their jobs and likely struggle to find a new one, since most companies are now focused on streamlining their operations rather than expanding their workforce.

Therefore, I expect us to see a new boom in small business formation over the coming years, many of which will require self-storage space.

Finally, self-storage is one of the most management-intensive property sectors. Leases are short, tenant turnover is high, pricing models are complex, and online marketing strategies are crucial to secure tenants.

Therefore, I expect AI to have a much bigger positive impact on the management of self-storage than most other property sectors.

It has the potential to boost occupancy rates thanks to more powerful, highly personalized AI-powered marketing, all while also increasing average rates thanks to more sophisticated pricing models, and cutting down costs, resulting in higher margins and cash flow.

All in all, this could be a game-changer for the self-storage industry over the coming years.

Even then, no one seems to have considered this narrative just yet.

Most investors are focused on the current oversupply and the weak demand that's caused by housing unaffordability reducing mobility. It is leading to stagnating or even slightly declining cash flows for major self-storage REITs and has depressed their valuations to some of the lowest ever recovered.

But real estate is cycling.

The current demand/supply dynamics are poor right now, but we think that conditions will already be much stronger in a year or two from now.

New supply is dropping rapidly as developers have gotten hurt, and most new projects are not economically feasible anymore. At the same time, demand is expected to strongly recover due to the AI revolution leading to more mobility and small business formations. I also think that the declining interest rates will benefit housing affordability, leading to more transactions, mobility, and demand for self-storage over the coming years.

Therefore, now could be a great time to increase our exposure to self-storage REITs while we are still near the bottom of the cycle, expecting them to surprise the market to the upside over the coming years as growth accelerates and the narrative changes.

But which is the best opportunity right now?

Recently, our biggest self-storage REIT, Big Yellow Group (BYG / BYLOF), surged in value after it was reported that Blackstone (BX), the world's leading private equity firm, was considering buying out the company.

By the way, ask yourself why Blackstone is suddenly becoming interested in pouring billions into self-storage, despite having little experience in this property sector. I think that it is likely because of what we just previously described here. Blackstone's leaders have spent a lot of time lately talking about how AI changes everything, and they likely see how it could help this particular property sector.

I think that Big Yellow remains undervalued, but it likely isn't the best pick right now, following the recent surge.

Our Australian top pick, National Storage REIT (NSR / NTSGF), also surged recently after Brookfield (BN) offered to buy out the company at a large premium. Brookfield is the second-largest alternative asset manager, just after Blackstone. This is yet another strong signal that self-storage REITs are undervalued, and the market is overlooking something important.

It is still underdeveloped, but likely not the best pick either.

Our Canadian self-storage REIT, StorageVault (SVAUF) (SVI:CA) is also up a lot lately, and rightfully so. It was deeply undervalued, but not so much anymore.

This leaves us with our US Top Pick, CubeSmart (CUBE), and our European Top Pick, Shurgard (SHUR / SSSAF), both of which have only gotten more attractive since our last updates, as their fundamentals have shown signs of improvement, but their share prices have kept dipping lower.

CubeSmart just recently reported what its CEO described as a "very solid third quarter" and slightly increased its full-year guidance. It noted diminishing headwinds from new supply with a steady, slow stabilization ahead. The market did not seem to like the use of the word "slow", which seems to imply that 2026 likely will be another slow year in terms of growth, but if you can look beyond that, growth is expected to strongly accelerate thereafter, which I expect to eventually be rewarded with a higher multiple.

Right now, CubeSmart is trading at just 13.5x FFO and offering a near 6% dividend yield. That's the lowest valuation in its peer group (PSA; EXR; NSA), despite owning the best properties and enjoying the best long-term growth prospects in our opinion.

We expect about 50% upside as it eventually returns closer to 20x FFO post-growth acceleration and rate cuts.

The main risk to the thesis is that the property sector remains oversupplied for longer than expected in the US. We think that conditions will improve already later this year, but if it takes longer and rent growth stagnates, it could take longer for CubeSmart to recover.

The story of Shurgard is very similar. Growth is limited right now due to oversupply and weak demand caused by low mobility. But a strong acceleration is expected in the next 1-2 years, which we expect to lead to a powerful repricing, given that it currently trades near its lowest valuation ever.

The main difference here is that Shurgard has even better long-term external growth prospects, as the European self-storage market is still 20 years behind the US, with way less storage space per capita, but the concept is now gaining in popularity.

In the next two years alone, it expects its development pipeline to increase its portfolio by about 14%, and that does not consider any organic growth from rising occupancy rates and rents.

It is also the only local player of scale in Europe with a BBB+ rated balance sheet and a low 23% LTV, which puts it in a strong position to potentially close another M&A deal over the coming years, as it finishes integrating its recent UK acquisition. This could further boost its growth and flip the narrative on Shurgard.

We think that it has about as much upside potential as CubeSmart, but offers better long-term growth potential with a lower dividend yield of 3.9%

The main risk about Shurgard is that they expand too fast, run into cost overruns on their development projects, and this leads to poor returns on invested capital. Historically, they have been very successful at developing these properties, so I am not too worried about this.

I think that the best risk-to-reward is achieved by owning both as part of a well-diversified self-storage portfolio, and that's what we are doing at High Yield Landlord as we attempt to beat the REIT sector averages by being selective.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 10 Best CEFs This Month: Average Yield Of 8.7% (January 2026)
data.description:
Introduction:

During the last two months of 2025, the market was mostly flat and traded in a tight range between S&P 500 (SPX) levels 6600 and 6900. However, the first two weeks of 2026 have been decent so far. It also made new highs a couple of times in the first week of this year, and the SPX level 7000 is not out of reach. The bull market is widely expected to continue in 2026, but there are a lot of things happening all around, especially geopolitically. We may have a new tariff tantrum that is just starting with Europe in focus over the issue of Greenland. The market can have a big negative swing in a hurry, especially when the valuations are so rich. However, the new theme for 2026 is that the foreign markets are expected to perform better (than the U.S. markets), similar to the trend that started in 2025. The main reason may be that they are still much cheaper in terms of valuations compared to the S&P500. So, it may be wise to diversify somewhat and have some allocations to foreign developed markets, and possibly a small allocation to the emerging markets.

Lastly (but not the least), the AI rally appears to be in the bubble territory, due to overspending and over-borrowing, but as they say, bubbles can last longer than most people think. Even then, it is certainly a reason to adopt a cautious approach.

The further progress and direction of the market may also depend on the actions of the Fed. You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

All that being said, it is always hard to find anything that has both the quality and the low valuation, so buying the best of the income investments regularly in small quantities is the key. As always, investors have to tread carefully amid all the uncertainties.

Why CEFs?

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal level of yield of less than 1.10%. Income investors and retirees can't survive on that level of income unless they have a very large pool of capital. Also, the method of raising income by selling shares is generally very stressful and full of pitfalls, and that's why it is not recommended. Alternatively, one can find solid blue-chip individual dividend stocks that pay much higher dividends than the S&P 500. They can also provide market-matching growth on a longer-term basis. To identify such stocks, we also publish a monthly article on the '5 Best DGI stocks'. You can find the most recent one here.

However, with CEFs, we can take the income to the next level. That said, they generally come with higher risks (discussed later), so we believe that an allocation of 20% to 30% to a diversified CEF portfolio should be good enough for most income investors. In summary, these investments should be used primarily for income, not growth.

We publish this monthly article on CEFs (Closed-end funds) to provide our readers with a select list of 10 CEFs that, as a group, provide wide diversification in terms of underlying assets, solid long-term performance at least on a relative basis, and are trading at decent valuations. Above all, the income goal for the group is to provide a yield of at least 8% or higher.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of 8.70% (as of 01/16/2026). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 17.7%, 12.2%, and 13.3% (annualized) in the last three, five years, and 10 years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is attractive, at -7.5%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Why Is It Important To Have A Diversified CEF Portfolio?

We believe a well-diversified CEF portfolio should contain at least 10 CEFs, preferably from different asset classes. It's also advisable to build the portfolio over a period rather than invest in one lump sum. If you were to invest in one CEF every month for a year, you would have a well-diversified CEF portfolio by the year's end. What we provide here every month is a list of ten probable candidates for further research. A CEF portfolio can be an important component of the overall portfolio strategy. One should preferably have a DGI portfolio as the foundation, and the CEF portfolio could be used to boost the income level to the desired level. How much should one allocate to CEFs? Each investor needs to answer this question himself/herself based on their personal situation and factors like the size of the portfolio, income needs, risk appetite, or risk tolerance.

Criteria to Shortlist:

Criteria

Brings down the number of funds to...

Reason for the Criteria

Baseline expense < 2.5% and Avg. Daily Volume > 10,000

Approx. 435 Funds

We do not want funds that charge excessive fees. Also, we want funds that have fair liquidity.

Market capitalization> 100 Million

Approx. 400 Funds

We do not want funds that are too small.

Track record/ History longer than five years (inception date 2016 or earlier)

Approx. 375 Funds

We want funds that have a reasonably long track record.

Discount/Premium < +7%

Approx. 350 Funds

We do not want to pay too high a premium; we want bigger discounts.

Distribution (dividend) Rate > 5%

Approx. 250-290 Funds

The current distribution (income) is reasonably high.

5-Year Annualized Return on NAV > 0% AND/ OR

3-Year Annualized Return on NAV >0%

Approx. 200-250 Funds

We want funds with a reasonably good past track record in maintaining their NAVs.

After we applied the above criteria this month, we are generally left with around 250 funds (plus or minus 5%) on our list. From here on, we apply our other criteria to narrow the list.

Note: All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article is sourced from Cefconnect.com, Cefa.com, and Morningstar.com.

Narrowing Down To 50 Funds:

To narrow down the number of funds to a more manageable count, we will shortlist ten funds based on each of the following criteria. Thereafter, we will apply certain qualitative criteria to each fund and rank them to select the top five.

At this stage, we also eliminate certain funds with substantially negative NAV returns for both three-year and five-year periods.

Seven Broad Criteria:

  • Excess discount/premium (explained below).
  • Distribution rate.
  • Return on NAV, last three years (medium-term).
  • Return on NAV, last five years (long term).
  • Coverage ratio.
  • Excess return over distributions.
  • The total weight (calculated up to this point).

Excess Discount/Premium

We certainly like funds that are offering large discounts (not premiums) to their NAVs. But what's important is to look at the "excess discount/premium" and not the absolute value. We want to see the discount (or premium) relative to their past record, say, a 52-week average.

Excess Discount/Premium = Current Discount/Premium (Minus) 52-Wk Avg. Discount/ Premium.

For more explanation of the excess discount and z-scores, please refer to our blog post (link provided at the beginning of the article).

We sort our list (of approx. 250 funds) on the "excess discount/premium" in descending order. For this criterion, the lower the value, the better it is. So, we select the top 10 funds (with the most negative values) from this sorted list.

(All data as of 01/16/2026)

Table 1:

High Current Distribution Rate

After all, most investors invest in CEF funds for their juicy distributions. Even though we should not chase yield, at this stage, we want to give them a fair representation in our list and subject them to a more rigorous criterion later on. We sorted our list on the current distribution rate (descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds from this sorted list.

Table 2:

Medium-Term Return on NAV (last three years)

We then sort our list on a three-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and select the top 10 funds. For this reason, we make a shortlist based on the total score and keep only a few of them on this list.

Table 3:

Five-Year Annualized Return on NAV

We then sorted our list on the five-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 4:

Coverage Ratio (Distributions Vs. Earnings)

The coverage ratio is derived by dividing the earnings per share by the distribution amount for a specific period. Please note that in some cases, the coverage ratio may be a bit inaccurate since the "earnings per share" may be three to six months old. But in most cases, it's fairly accurate. Furthermore, the coverage ratio is not critical for pure equity funds as they generate very little dividend income, and they cover most of their distributions from capital gains. However, we sorted our list by coverage ratio and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 5:

Excess Return Over Distribution

If a fund provides a very high distribution but low returns over 3, 5, or 10-year periods, it does not help the long-term interests of the investor. In simple terms, the fund is just overpaying the distributions and hurting the NAV in the long term. That is why we calculate a factor we like to call "excess return." This excess return is what is provided by the fund over and above the distribution rate over a specified time period. We calculate it by subtracting the distribution rate from the average of the three-year and five-year NAV returns. In this list, we included the top 10 funds.

Table 6:

Total Weight (Quality Score) Calculated Up to This Point

Note: The Total Weight calculation is not fully completed at this point since we have not considered the 10-year NAV return. However, we select the top 15 names based on current calculations.

Table 7:

From the selections so far, we have 75 funds in total (tables 1-7).

We will need to see if there are any duplicates among them. In our current list of 75 funds, there were 26 duplicates, meaning there are funds that appeared more than once. The following names appear twice (or more):

Appeared twice: ADX, AIO, EDF, EOD, GAM, HYT, and XFLT (7 duplicates).

Appeared three times: CSQ, EIC, EMO, PDX, and TWN (10 duplicates).

Appeared four times: EOS, GGN, and JCE (9 duplicates).

Once we remove 26 duplicate rows, we are left with 49 (75- 26) funds.

Note: It may be worthwhile to mention here that just because a fund has appeared multiple times does not necessarily make it an attractive candidate. Sometimes, a fund may appear multiple times simply for the wrong reasons, like a high current discount, a high excess discount, or a very high distribution rate that may not be sustainable. However, during the second filtering stage, it may not score well on the overall quality score due to other factors, like a poor track record. That said, if a fund has appeared four times or more, it may be worth a second look.

Narrowing Down to Just 10 Funds:

In our list of funds, we may already have some of the best probable candidates. However, so far, they have been selected based on one (or two) criteria that each may be good at. That's not nearly enough. So, we will apply a combination of criteria by applying weights to eight factors to calculate the total quality score and filter out the best ones.

We will apply weights to each of the eight criteria:

Once we have calculated the weights, we combine them to calculate the "Total Combined Weight," also called the "Quality Score."

The sorted list (spreadsheet) of 49 funds on the "combined total weight or quality score" is attached here.

File-for-export_-_10_Best_CEFs_-_Jan_2026.xlsx

10-Positions Portfolio of The Month:

Before we get to the final ten, we will narrow down our list to 20 names. So, we sorted our table based on the sector and then the quality score. Furthermore, in this round of selections, we mostly pick one or two top funds from each sector (based on quality score). There is a bit of subjective judgment, as we tend to prioritize funds that pay regular and consistent distributions on a monthly or quarterly basis. We also weed out any fund that may have a very poor track record in terms of NAV performance. Funds that may have inconsistent dividends (even if they are high) generally do not make it to our top list. Furthermore, be aware that many times, single-country funds score high in our rankings, but most of them pay variable dividends on an annual or semi-annual basis. In addition, being single-country funds, they can be inherently riskier since their future returns are tied to just one country, be it economic, regulatory, or geopolitical factors. For these reasons, they generally do not make it to our top 5 lists, but they do appear on our list of 20. Individual investors could consider them based on their goals.

Here's the list of the top 20 selections (from different asset classes) in the order of sector:

Table 8:

10-Best-CEFs of the Month

In the end, if we only want to select ten funds, we will need to apply our subjective judgment to some degree. While we narrow down this list to ten names, we would be mindful to keep the list as diversified as possible in terms of sectors and asset classes. Since this step is somewhat subjective, the choice would differ from person to person. Nonetheless, here are the selections for this month, based on our perspective (in order of Quality Score):

Here's the list of the top 10 selections this month (in order of sector):

(EOS), (AIO), (EIC), (EOD), (PDX), (JCE), (CSQ), (RNP), (PEO), and (CCD).

Table-9:

Some information about the selections:

We will only briefly describe the selected funds. Since there are 10 of them, we will not be providing a very detailed analysis of them.

Here are some details on many of our selections:

(EOS) Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income II:

When it comes to covered call funds, it is hard to beat Eaton Vance funds. Also, EOS is one of the best funds in its category. Moreover, its high exposure to the technology sector has definitely helped the fund to shine in the last 10 years. It currently holds only about 50 companies and overwrites (sells options) roughly 50% of its portfolio to generate income and reduce volatility. The distribution yield is attractive at 8.1%, and currently offers a decent discount of -7%. The fund uses no leverage, and its expenses are comparable to those of its competitors. The past performance record is solid, and it has returned 25%, 11%, and 14.8% (annualized) over the last 3, 5, and 10-year periods.

(AIO) Virtus AI & Tech Opp Fund:

This is a fund focused on AI (Artificial Intelligence) and technology companies. But it comes with a unique flavor of combining both the equity and convertible securities, as well as some bonds, based on market conditions. Please note the fund has a limited term until 2031 (extendable by 18 months). The fund also uses modest leverage and has performed exceedingly well in the last three years. It provides a decent distribution yield of just under 8% and is currently trading at a decent discount of roughly -7%. This fund was included last month as well, and the price has gone up somewhat since then, so the yield is slightly lower.

(EIC) Eagle Point Income Co:

EIC is probably the riskiest fund in this selection of 10 funds. The fund uses high leverage of 31% in the form of preferred stock. The fund primarily invests in junior debt tranches of CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations). In addition, it may invest up to 35% of total assets in CLO equity securities and related securities.

Due to the decline in interest rates, similar to its peers, the fund has been reducing its distribution amount. The monthly distribution has come down from $0.20 a share to $0.11, a 45% reduction. This has caused the price to fall during most of 2025, and that has continued this year, but the pace has slowed down. So, it has been brutal for someone who acquired the shares prior to 2024. However, the yield is still very attractive for new buyers at 11.8%, in spite of the lower distribution amount. The discount has also widened to -15.6%. With reduced distribution, the current distribution is fully covered by the NII (net investment income). However, on a full nine-month basis (January to September), it only covered to the extent of 75%, as the distribution was much higher at the beginning of last year. We think that after significant correction, the price and yield are attractive. However, it is quite likely that the distribution amount may further fall as the interest rates decline even more, so this fund should only be accumulated in multiple lots on a dollar-cost-average basis.

(EOD) Allspring Global Dividend Opportunity Fund:

The international funds of all types have done well during the year 2025, and that trend is continuing in 2026. This fund is selected to provide exposure to global equity as well as fixed income. The fund is invested roughly 80% in global equities with a focus on dividends, and the rest in fixed income (18%) and cash equivalents (2%). The equity portion is invested in 70-80 companies spread across many sectors and segments. The fund also employs an options strategy on a portion of the equity portfolio to enhance income and to generate gains on call options. The fund also uses a modest leverage of just under 15%.

The allocation to the U.S. is roughly 65% of the total, and the rest is invested in countries such as the U.K., France, Japan, China, Germany, Italy, Canada, Taiwan, and Brazil. The largest sector is information Technology at 25%, followed by Financials and Communications.

(PDX) PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund:

As we did last month, we are including PDX, one of PIMCO's newest but popular funds, in our top 10. This fund regularly appears near the top in multiple categories. The reason it stands out is that it has performed remarkably well in the last three and five years. The fund is only about six years old, so it does not have a long history. Some of its past performance can be attributed to its timing of inception (in 2019) and initial allocation to the energy sector. In fact, this fund was known as "PIMCO Energy & Tactical Credit Opportunities fund (ticker - NRGX)" until Nov.2023. Since the beginning of 2024, PIMCO has decided to diversify the fund from an energy-focused fund to a mix of energy and multi-sector Credit/Bonds. Unlike other PIMCO funds, its distribution yield is a bit low but has gone up significantly to over 8%, due to the ongoing correction in price. PIMCO has aggressively raised the dividends in the last two years and has also paid special year-end distributions. The fund is currently offering an attractive discount of nearly -10% compared to premiums on other similar PIMCO funds.

(JCE) Nuveen Core Equity Alpha

Under the U.S. equity category, we are selecting JCE this month, though General American Investors (GAM) and Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) are also equally attractive. That said, for long-term holding, we would prefer ADX out of the three.

The fund pays quarterly, and its distribution amount is usually consistent, unlike GAM. JCE’s current yield is attractive at 8.01%, and the discount is decent at -6% (better than long-term averages).

The primary investment objective of the fund is to seek capital appreciation, with income only a secondary objective. The fund invests mostly in large-capitalized core stocks with growth potential at reasonable valuations. The fund does not use leverage, but writes call options on a portion of the portfolio. It holds roughly 110 equity stocks. The fund has shown excellent performance of 22.4%, 14.1%, and 13.3% over the last 3, 5, and 10 years.

(CSQ) Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund:

This fund is a hybrid fund and invests primarily in a diversified portfolio of equities, convertible securities, and high-yield corporate bonds. Nearly 66% of CSQ's capital is invested in equities, and the rest is in convertibles, bonds, and preferred securities. Also, the equity is a bit concentrated in tech (but lower than the S&P500) and other growth-oriented stocks, but it does represent other sectors reasonably well. Performance-wise, since 2005, it has nearly matched the returns of the S&P500, assuming all distributions were reinvested. With 1/3rd of the portfolio in fixed income, we would expect CSQ to underperform the S&P500, but it achieves similar performance by using significant leverage of roughly 30%. The leverage increases the volatility and drawdowns somewhat higher than those of the S&P500. It recently increased the distribution amount, and the current yield is quite decent at 7.55%.

(RNP) Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Inc Fund:

This is a hybrid fund and provides nearly identical exposure to real estate equity, mostly REITs, and the other half to preferred and fixed income securities. The fund also uses significant leverage of over 30%. The real estate sector had a tough few years due to the elevated interest rates. But with the expectations of continued decline in interest rates, the sector may experience a recovery in 2026, as has been the case during the first two weeks of 2026. The fund currently offers a moderate discount of roughly -4.6% and a decent distribution yield of 8.1%.

(PEO) Adams Natural Resources Fund:

If you like to own some of the large-cap energy majors at a discount, you can't beat the PEO fund offered by the Adams group. It is a very nice fund to own the major oil companies at a discount (currently hovering around -9%). This fund provides a diversified exposure to large energy companies. At the same time, there is some over-concentration at the top, given the fact that the two oil majors, Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), together represent nearly 37% of the total assets. In addition, it currently provides an attractive yield of 8.4%. Please keep in mind that this is an energy sector-specific fund, and hence, one should allocate accordingly.

(CCD) Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income:

The Fund primarily invests in a portfolio of convertible securities, debt securities, and other income-producing securities, including some equities. Investments include common and preferred stocks, investment-grade and below-investment-grade bonds, loans, equity-linked notes, and floating-rate securities. It also uses high leverage exceeding 30%. This fund was selected last month as well. Since then, it has appreciated somewhat, and the yield has gone down a bit. But it still pays a yield of 10.4% on current prices. The distribution is paid monthly. The shares are trading at a small but insignificant premium and compare favorably with their very high premiums over the last 12 months and 3 years. The lower interest rates expected in the future should help this fund. However, on the negative side, there is some chance that the management may reduce the distribution payout as it has a negative income from operations (NII + realized and unrealized capital gains). So, investors need to be somewhat cautious and use due diligence.

CEF-Specific Investment Risks:

It goes without saying that CEFs generally have some additional risks. This section is specifically relevant for investors who are new to CEF investing, but in general, all CEF investors should be aware of it. For more details on these specific risks, please read our blog post on CEFs.

  • Use of high leverage and high fees
  • High volatility (compared to the broader market)
  • Premium over NAVs (market price higher than the NAV)
  • Asset-specific concentration risk.

Concluding Thoughts:

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal yield of less than 1.20%. Many income investors and retirees can't survive on that kind of income. For income investors, we recommend building a core bucket of DGI (Dividend growth) stocks and supplementing it with a basket of CEFs to increase the overall income.

We use our screening process to highlight the best closed-end funds for investment each month. Our filtering process demands that our selections have an above-average long-term performance record, offer an average of roughly 8% distributions, and are relatively cheaper with reasonable discounts. We have also provided a larger list of ten CEFs from many different asset classes.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of 8.70% (as of 01/16/2026). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 17.7%, 12.2%, and 13.3% (annualized) in the last three, five years, and 10 years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is attractive, at -7.5%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Please note that these selections are based on our proprietary rating system and are dynamic in nature. So, they can change from month to month (or even week to week). At the same time, some funds can repeat from one month to the next if they remain attractive over an extended period. Furthermore, note that if a fund has not made it to our list, it does not mean it is not good. It could be because they may not be attractively priced at this time or trading at a premium while running our filtering process.
